artifacts.content:
Hi Isa,

I’m hiding in the Civic Center break room for ten minutes before the 3:00 p.m. South River/Willow Creek traffic meeting and needed to brain-dump to someone who won’t tell me to “just breathe.”

Work first: the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village is swallowing my life. It’s still technically AG-20 in Sutter County but the City’s General Plan has it as Mixed Use Neighborhood (8–18 du/ac), and Mark keeps “reminding” me of that every time I push back on density. At our one‑on‑one on November 6, 2023 he flat-out tied bumping the assumed density above 18 du/ac to closing the $2.3M gap on the South River Road sewer trunk and hinted my last performance review and the time I took off for Sofia’s anxiety appointments at Feather River Behavioral Health could “come up” if I wasn’t “flexible.” I wrote it all down in my work journal because it felt gross.

Meanwhile at home, Daniel’s March 2024 DUI on Garden Highway in Yuba City is still haunting us. His license suspension means I’m the only driver, so every weekday I’m doing the 7:30 a.m. run in the Pilot: Mateo to Willow Creek Elementary, Sofia to Willow Creek Middle, then downtown to 2450 Civic Center Drive. On site‑visit days I’m begging Mark to let me start a little later so I can get them dropped off.

And you know Mom—after her March 2024 eviction scare and that heat exhaustion episode at Adventist Rideout, I keep looking at those 14 affordable units in the project and wondering if we could eventually get her into one. I know I have to keep that separate from my job, but it’s hard not to picture her there instead of in that leaking Gray Avenue place.

Anyway, I have to run. Text me after your shift? I miss having you close enough for coffee on days like this.

Besos,
Laura

metadata.subject: Quick vent before another South River meeting

artifacts.content:
Hi Daniel,

I just got home from Yuba City and everyone’s finally asleep, so I’m staring at the PG&E bill and trying not to cry. Between our place on Willow Creek Drive and Mamá’s apartment, we really did end up about $2,900 behind, just like I was afraid we would when I took that unpaid leave after her surgery at Rideout Regional. The $320‑per‑month payment plan I set up with PG&E in January 2024 is technically manageable, but it feels like this extra weight on top of everything else.

It’s funny (not in a ha‑ha way) how this bleeds into work. I’m sitting in meetings talking about the Mixed-Use Village’s higher electricity demand and energy mitigation while our own lights feel like they’re hanging by a thread. I keep thinking about that January 2023 flood at Mamá’s mobile home park in Yuba City, when the storm drains and basins were overwhelmed and she had to be evacuated. That’s why I’m obsessed with the dual‑purpose stormwater quality/detention basins on this project—oversized, real low‑impact development features, not just pretty bioswales on paper.

And then there’s Sofia. I still see her in the Willow Creek Middle School parking lot last fall, shaking after her panic attack, or back in October 2022 when she saw the aftermath of that bike accident in front of the school. Between that and Mateo’s asthma, every time we talk about air quality and traffic near the Feather River corridor, it’s our kids’ faces I picture, not anonymous “sensitive receptors.”

I know we’ll get through the arrears. I just needed you to know why I seem so intense about detention basins and energy use in those staff meetings—it’s not abstract for me.

Love,
L

metadata.subject: PG&E, storms, and why I’m so keyed up about this project

artifacts.content:
Work/Personal Reflection – Late night at dining table

I’m trying to make sense of how tangled this Riverbend Mixed-Use Village has become with our actual lives.

Transportation: On paper, I talk about reducing VMT with Class II bike lanes and multi‑use trails, but in reality I’m still driving our 2012 silver Honda Civic EX from 812 Willow Creek Drive to City Hall most days. Ever since that rear‑end collision on Highway 99 near the East 20th Street exit in Chico back in January 2023, my hip hasn’t been the same. From March–May 2023, Dr. Karen Soto at Enloe Rehabilitation Center kept telling me to keep bike rides under 10–15 minutes, which is laughable for a real commute.

Then Daniel’s March 2024 DUI stop on Garden Highway in Yuba City and the six‑month license suspension shoved everything onto my shoulders: the 7:30 a.m. loop to Willow Creek Elementary and Willow Creek Middle in the Pilot, then out to South River Road/Willow Creek Drive for field visits. When the Civic’s $480 registration renewal came due right after Mamá Elena’s June 2024 gallbladder surgery at Adventist Health and the $900 brake repair on the Pilot, I started carpooling from Javier’s shop in Marysville for site meetings because I refused to drive an unregistered car to a city project.

Meanwhile, Mateo’s March 2023 asthma hospitalization at Enloe and Dr. Patel’s comments about high‑pollution days along the Feather River corridor are in the back of my mind every time I read the air quality chapter. I keep telling myself these personal stories can’t show up in official documents, but they’re the real reasons I push so hard on mitigation.

Note to self: bring all of this up with Dr. Alvarez next session—this isn’t just “work stress.”

metadata.title: Reflection – cars, money, kids, and this project

artifacts.content:
Hi Javi,

Thanks again for letting me stash my car at your shop and hitch rides in your truck to the South River Road/Willow Creek Drive site since August. With the Civic’s $480 registration renewal still hanging out there after Mamá’s June 2024 gallbladder surgery at Adventist and that $900 brake job on the Pilot, I just can’t justify driving it to official meetings when it’s technically not registered.

I was thinking about all of this today while reviewing the “Other Development” coordination for the project. Remember the sewer main backup into our house on Willow Creek Drive in February 2023? Mateo’s GI infection, the photos of sewage in the hallway, Mark Ellison signing off on that $18,700 emergency contract so Public Works could dig up the line by the subdivision—now the same replacement is being folded into the backbone infrastructure package for the Mixed-Use Village. It’s surreal to see our nightmare as a line item.

And then there’s Dad. When I talk about extending the 12‑inch water main in South River Road, I keep flashing back to August 2022 when he helped troubleshoot that E. coli contamination incident in the looped system by the Riverbend industrial tract. Officially I can’t connect the two, but personally it’s why I’m so adamant about redundancy and water quality monitoring.

On the non‑infrastructure front: I’m still coaching Sofia’s rec soccer at the Willow Creek Middle fields three nights a week (June–August 2024 felt like one long car shuttle), and I’m trying to keep up with aqua‑Zumba at the Riverbend Community Pool on Willow Creek Drive twice a week since my A1C hit 6.4 in November 2023. So if I look half‑asleep when you pick me up, that’s why.

Love you. Tell the guys at the shop I owe them pan dulce.

Laura

metadata.subject: Trucks, sewers, and why I’m so stubborn about this project

artifacts.content:
Event: Saturday Willow Creek loop + moms’ check‑in
Date: 2024-03-09
Time: 8:00–9:30 AM
Location: Willow Creek riparian trail behind Willow Creek Middle School
Attendees: Laura; Ana; Maribel; Rosa; rotating others

Notes:
- Meet at the Willow Creek Middle parking lot, stretch, then do our usual 3–4 mile walk along the riparian trail.
- Bring the breathing exercise script Dr. Alvarez at Riverbend Behavioral Health Clinic gave me after my panic attack at the City’s Fall Festival in October 2022—several of the moms asked me to run through it again.
- Quick safety reminder at the trailhead: no one walks alone. Since that July 2023 incident when a man followed me from the Willow Creek Elementary parking lot to the second fitness station and I had to file a report with Riverbend PD, I’m only comfortable being out here in a group.
- Check in with Sofia about whether she wants to join us for the last 10–15 minutes to practice being near bikes again. She still refuses to ride near South River Road after seeing the aftermath of the October 2022 bike accident in front of Willow Creek Middle; Ms. Patel (school counselor) and Dr. Alvarez at Feather River Behavioral Health are working with her on the panic attacks.
- After the walk, maybe swing by Mamá’s in Yuba City to drop off groceries and remind her about her next follow‑up after the gallbladder surgery.

To bring: water, hat, inhaler for Mateo if he tags along, extra granola bars for the kids, printed flyers about the upcoming community movie night at the central green amphitheater.

metadata.title: Latina moms anxiety walk – Willow Creek

artifacts.content:
Hi Mami,

I wanted to write everything down so you don’t worry and so we remember the details for later.

Do you remember the December 2022 storms, when our side yard at 812 Willow Creek Drive flooded for three days? The water sat there so long that mold grew in Sofia’s back bedroom and her asthma really flared. I ended up emailing her pediatrician about it. That’s one of the reasons I’m pushing so hard for better stormwater detention basins and low‑impact development features in the new project near South River Road—we don’t want any other families dealing with ponding like that.

Then in February 2023, the sewer main backed up into our house. Poor Mateo got that gastrointestinal infection afterward. The City’s Public Works Director, Mark Ellison, had to sign an $18,700 emergency contract to replace the undersized line by the subdivision. Now that replacement is being coordinated with the Mixed-Use Village infrastructure. When I see the plans, I still smell the mess in our hallway.

I also keep thinking about you having to evacuate from the Yuba City mobile home park in January 2023 when the storm drains and basins there were overwhelmed. That’s exactly why I keep telling my coworkers that our dual‑purpose stormwater quality/detention basins can’t just meet the minimum—they need to handle the kind of intense runoff we’ve actually lived through.

I know all these numbers and dates sound very “planner,” but underneath it I’m just your daughter who doesn’t want sewage in the house, flooded yards, or you sitting in a shelter again like during the 1997 floods.

Te quiero mucho,
Laura

metadata.subject: Por qué estoy tan enfocada en el agua y las inundaciones

artifacts.content:
Hey Marisol,

Catching up between draft EIR edits and soccer practice chaos.

You’d appreciate how circular my life feels right now. I’m spending my days describing the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village’s parks and “community benefits,” then my evenings actually using those same kinds of spaces. From June through August 2024 I ended up coaching Sofia’s 8th‑grade girls’ team on the Willow Creek Middle School fields three nights a week because we just couldn’t swing the $425 club fees after Daniel’s unpaid leave in spring 2024. The free city rec program saved us.

On top of that, I finally listened to my doctor after my November 2023 labs came back with an A1C of 6.4 and creeping blood pressure. Since January 2024 I’ve been doing low‑impact aqua‑Zumba twice a week at the Riverbend Community Pool on Willow Creek Drive. It’s actually helping, and it’s also become this informal therapy group for stressed‑out moms.

Meanwhile, at work I’m writing about the 14 deed‑restricted affordable units like they’re just numbers, but in my head I’m running my parents’ Social Security math from that September 2021 memo—$3,200/month combined, target 2‑bedroom rent around $1,050 by the transit plaza—trying to picture Elena and Carlos aging somewhere that isn’t a leaking duplex or oven‑hot apartment. And every time I revise the transportation section, I still see Sofia in the crosswalk at Willow Creek Drive the morning of April 27, 2022, when that distracted driver rolled the stop while she was biking to school. That near‑miss is why I pushed so hard for the new signalized intersection and the Class I trail along Willow Creek.

So yeah, “complete streets” and “equitable access” sound very academic, but for me it’s just our messy little family, stretched across Riverbend and Yuba City, trying to survive.

Miss you. Come visit and I’ll show you the site—and the pool class.

Laura

metadata.subject: How the project and my life keep colliding
